justification: Chapter 2 has a dedicated dynastic succession section (lines 884-912) arguing that James stepping into leadership after Jesus, followed by Simon and others of the family, is the pattern of a dynasty rather than a spontaneous sect. The finding adds Tabor's description of James sitting on a throneos to pronounce binding decisions as a 'rabbi of the Nazarenes' — this is new textual/video evidence that directly strengthens Chapter 2's existing argument: James exercised institutional authority modeled on Hellenistic political leadership, not informal charismatic authority. Chapter 6 mentions James only in passing (lines 200-202, 277) in the context of political executions and martyrdom; it has no section on James's leadership style or succession role. The throneos detail is most directly useful to Chapter 2's case that the movement was organized as a dynastic institution.

Chapter 2 argues extensively that Jesus organized his movement along pre-existing Hellenistic institutional lines (ecclesia section, lines 360-480) and that James's succession was dynastic rather than improvised (dynastic succession section, lines 884-912). The finding adds evidence on two points that directly strengthen existing Chapter 2 arguments: (1) wealthy patron support at the Last Supper fits Chapter 2's argument that Jesus operated within elite Hellenistic civic-patronage structures, not a rural peasant economy; (2) James as institutional successor with Mary as matriarch fits Chapter 2's dynastic succession argument. Chapter 6 does not contain a section on early community organizational structure or house-church patronage; its focus is Byzantine restoration theology, phoenix symbolism, and martyrdom literature. Both pieces of evidence in this finding are most directly useful in Chapter 2.
